Hypothesis rejected – not purely group-based. 24.03.25 08:55 Analysis Azure AD Sign-In Logs For affected users: • Token contains claim for “COMPLIANCE_TEAM” • No claim for “Compliance_Core” • Conditional Access evaluation successful No blocking visible. 24.03.25 10:10 In-depth review via Microsoft Purview / Information Protection REPORT 8: # SharePoint folder access — Sensitivity Label vs ACL (Customer Company 25 AG / Compliance_Review_2025) This community centers on a recurring SharePoint access problem in Customer Company 25 AG where a folder (/Projects/Compliance_Review_2025) was granted unique SharePoint ACLs, then later had a Microsoft Information Protection sensitivity label applied. The label enforced encryption tied to a different Azure AD group (Compliance_Core) while the SharePoint ACL referenced SP_Compliance_Main (which in turn referenced a cloud-created COMPLIANCE_TEAM). Azure AD Connect synchronization of an On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM object with the same display name (different ObjectID) replaced or changed the cloud object evaluation, excluding cloud-only external guests and producing access loss. Token caching and token-claim timing caused the symptom to appear intermittent (users could temporarily access with stale tokens, then lose access when tokens refreshed). The issue was reproduced in a TEST TENANT, investigated using Azure AD sign-in logs and Microsoft Purview, escalated through HELPDESK → BU Infrastructure (M365/Identity) → System Engineers, remediated by group consolidation / full sync / reapply label / invalidate tokens, and validated by the customer's Audit Team and Consulting Sample Company. ## Primary technical root cause: label encryption permissions diverged from SharePoint ACLs because the label was applied after unique permissions and 'Preserve custom permissions' was not enabled The folder /Projects/Compliance_Review_2025 had Unique Permissions (a SharePoint ACL) applied, and the Sensitivity Label ("Confidential – Internal") was applied later; because the label was applied without enabling the "Preserve custom permissions" option, the label's encryption referenced a different Azure AD group (Compliance_Core) than the ACL did, producing a permissions divergence that prevented decryption for some users. This sequence (ACL → label) and the missing preserve option are explicitly documented in the incident analysis and relationships examined during the investigation. This misalignment meant that SharePoint could show an ACL granting access (SP_Compliance_Main) while the label-based encryption check denied decryption because the token claims did not show membership in Compliance_Core. The label-enforced encryption thus became the effective gatekeeper for content access in practice, overriding the expectation set by the ACL alone. The incident record links the folder, the label configuration and the preserve-permissions option directly to the observed inconsistent access behavior. [Data: Entities (550, 571, 575, 556); Relationships (981, 947, 980)] ## Azure AD Connect group/object collision excluded external guests when the synchronized On‑Prem group object took precedence Investigators found a cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM group (containing external guest accounts) and a separate On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM group (with the same display name but a different Object ID). Azure AD Connect synchronization behavior caused the synchronized On‑Prem group object to overwrite or replace the cloud object reference used by SharePoint, which excluded cloud-only guest members and caused external guests to lose access when SharePoint evaluated the synchronized group object. System Engineer 14 and the identity team reproduced and explained this behaviour using Microsoft Graph queries and by reviewing Azure AD Connect behavior. The remediation included consolidation and a full sync to restore the intended cloud group membership. This synchronization/ID collision is the proximate cause that converted the ACL/label divergence into an actual user outage for external guests. [Data: Entities (269, 265, 266, 267); Relationships (446, 437, 430)] ## Token caching and claim timing produced intermittent/partial access symptoms Multiple records indicate that cached or previously-issued authentication tokens contained older group claims (e.g., COMPLIANCE_TEAM) that temporarily allowed access until tokens were refreshed or re-evaluated — at which point the absence of the Compliance_Core claim caused decryption to fail. Browser session state (including Incognito vs normal sessions) and Office client sessions retained tokens that influenced who saw the folder and when. Azure AD sign-in logs and token/claim records showed affected tokens contained COMPLIANCE_TEAM claims but lacked Compliance_Core; sign-in log timing and Conditional Access evaluation events correlated with observed intermittent behavior. Engineers therefore combined group/object fixes with token invalidation guidance (full sign‑out from Office + browser) to force reissuance of tokens with current claims. The cached-token behavior explains the seemingly sporadic nature of the access failures. [Data: Entities (681, 570, 558, 574); Relationships (991, 962, 1204)] ## Issue was reproducible in an isolated TEST TENANT and client-session tests showed cache-related differences Engineers reproduced the same ACL→label sequence in a TEST TENANT by creating a folder with unique permissions and applying a Sensitivity Label after the ACL was set; the test confirmed that token renewal and timing produced the same temporary-allowed/then-denied behavior. Browser Incognito testing produced different/partial access compared to normal sessions, further implicating cached session tokens and client caching as modifiers of symptom presentation. The use of a non-production test tenant allowed safe reproduction of the sequence and validation of remediation steps before applying them to production, and test findings (including incognito behavior) were used to justify the combined remediation approach (group consolidation + full sync + token invalidation + label reapplication). [Data: Entities (503, 567, 262); Relationships (993, 955, 996)] ## This is a recurrent, multi-ticket operational problem with demonstrated business impact Multiple support incidents across March–April 2025 reference similar SharePoint access issues for Customer Company 25 AG (tickets TI-1571046, TI-1578891, TI-1583327) and a related Teams voice ticket for a different customer (TI-1609823). The Customer Company 25 AG incidents specifically documented external project members losing access to project folders and required escalation to BU Infrastructure – M365/Identity and system engineers for in‑depth remediation. The recurrence and the number of escalations indicate a systemic interaction between label application processes, group/object management and synchronization rules that goes beyond a single, transient misconfiguration — and therefore warrants process and architecture changes to prevent repeat outages. [Data: Entities (546, 256, 676, 189); Relationships (419, 934, 1180, 288)] ## Stakeholder and escalation pathways were established and executed, but clarified ownership of label application and group governance is needed The support workflow shows HELPDESK as the frontline intake and triage point, escalating to BU Infrastructure – M365/Identity and then to System Engineers 11 and 14 for deeper analysis; Consulting Sample Company performed the investigation and remediation and the customer's Audit Team validated the final state. Communication paths (helpdesk ↔ contact persons, identity BU ↔ system engineers, consulting vendor ↔ customer) were used effectively to resolve the incidents. However, the root cause — interplay between SharePoint ACLs, sensitivity label configuration, and Azure AD group/object management — crosses service, identity and compliance boundaries. This cross-domain ownership means that explicit, codified governance is required for label application (who may apply labels and how), group naming/object‑ID collision handling, and Azure AD Connect change control in order to avoid reintroducing the same fault through routine administrative changes. [Data: Entities (85, 549, 261, 548, 568, 572); Relationships (297, 944, 967, 971)] ## Security/compliance configuration nuance: labels enforced encryption but ACL/label misalignment can deny intended principals Sensitivity Labels managed through Microsoft Purview/AIP enforce encryption that is evaluated against Azure AD group membership (e.g., requiring membership in Compliance_Core). The label mechanism is therefore capable of blocking decryption even when SharePoint ACLs indicate permission. In this incident, applying the label without preserving custom ACL mapping effectively changed the authority model for access, producing potential availability impacts for legitimate users and creating a risk that compliance-related labels could be misapplied in ways that disrupt business processes. The investigation used Purview tooling to review label configuration and concluded that label application options (such as "Preserve custom permissions") materially affect access outcomes; thus label application must be treated as both a security and availability control, and require pre‑application checks and a rollback/test path. [Data: Entities (557, 559, 556); Relationships (977, 959, 981)] ## Prioritized, actionable recommendations to prevent recurrence Based on the documented causes and remediation steps, the following prioritized actions are indicated: (1) Treat label application as a controlled change — enable "Preserve custom permissions" or explicitly map ACL → label group mappings and test in a TEST TENANT prior to production; (2) Implement Azure AD Connect governance: detect display-name collisions, require ObjectID reconciliation/explicit mapping for groups with identical display names, and use pre-sync validation to prevent cloud group overwrite; (3) Add a runbook to force token revocation and clear Office/browser sessions after any group or label change (sign‑out guidance, token revocation, and customer communication); (4) Maintain a pre-label checklist (verify group referenced by label exists and contains intended principals including guests) and require Purview review/approval for applied encryption labels; (5) Add monitoring/alerts for rapid detection: track label application events, group-object replacements, and anomalous sign-in errors referencing encryption failures. Each recommended action maps directly to evidence and remediation steps recorded in the incident (preserve permissions option, Azure AD Connect behavior, test-tenant verification, token invalidation guidance, and Purview analysis) and should be implemented in the order of operational impact and ease of deployment (preserve-permissions / pre-application tests first, then AAD Connect governance and automation). [Data: Entities (575, 269, 503, 681, 559); Relationships (980, 446, 993, 1204, 940)]

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: . Why this explains “same link, same user, different outcome” - Sensitivity labels that enforce encryption require the user’s token to carry the group/claim the label allows (decrypt rights). If the label references Compliance_Core but SharePoint ACLs reference SP_Compliance_Main / COMPLIANCE_TEAM (different AAD objects), a user can be shown as permitted by SharePoint yet be unable to decrypt the files controlled by the label. Token caching (old vs new token claims) produces the incognito vs normal‑session difference you observed

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 64: # Azure AD Connect vs Cloud COMPLIANCE_TEAM — External Guest Access Loss (SharePoint) This community centers on a cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM group and an On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM group that share a display name but have distinct ObjectIDs. Azure AD Connect synchronized the On‑Prem group into Entra ID/Azure AD, which progressively replaced or caused SharePoint to evaluate the synchronized On‑Prem object instead of the cloud-native group. Because external guest accounts lived only in the cloud-native group (they were not present On‑Prem), those external guests lost SharePoint access while internal users (present in the On‑Prem group) retained access. A system engineer reproduced the issue, used Microsoft Graph and the Entra ID portal to compare group membership and source attributes, and guided remediation steps including consolidation/cleanup and a full sync to restore intended access mappings. [Data: Entities (265,266,267,268,269,270); Relationships (437,440,441,445,446,435,430)] ## Root cause: Azure AD Connect synchronization caused the cloud-native group to be overwritten/evaluated as the On‑Prem synchronized object, removing external guests from effective membership Investigators observed that Azure AD Connect gradually overwrote or deactivated the cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM group with the synchronized On‑Prem group (same display name, different Object IDs), which changed the effective membership evaluated by SharePoint and resulted in external guests losing access. This behavior is documented in the service description for Azure AD Connect and the relationship between the cloud and On‑Prem group objects. [Data: Entities (269,265,266); Relationships (437,440,446)] The critical aspect is the display-name collision combined with synchronization semantics: SharePoint was referencing the synchronized On‑Prem object (the object with the On‑Prem ObjectID), not the separate cloud-native group that contained guest members, so membership evaluation excluded cloud-only guest accounts. This mismatch between object identity and display name is the immediate mechanism of access loss. [Data: Entities (265,266,267); Relationships (437,444,435)] ## Scope and impact: External guests lost access while internal users remained unaffected External project members provisioned as Guest accounts in Entra ID/Azure AD lost SharePoint access because they were members of the cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM, which was distinct from the synchronized On‑Prem group used by SharePoint. Internal users were members of the On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM and therefore continued to have access. This differential outcome is captured by the entity relationships showing guest membership in the cloud group and internal membership in the On‑Prem group. [Data: Entities (267,268,265,266); Relationships (437,441)] Investigators also noted that affected accounts had differing "source" attributes visible in the Entra ID portal; examining the source attribute helped correlate which accounts originated On‑Prem versus in the cloud, which was instrumental in identifying why some users were impacted and others were not. This observation ties the access symptoms back to identity source metadata in Entra ID. [Data: Entities (270,267); Relationships (445)] ## Investigation and diagnostics used: System Engineer 14, Microsoft Graph, and Entra ID inspection A system engineer conducted in-depth analysis: reviewing Azure AD Connect behavior, inspecting group membership via Microsoft Graph, reproducing the issue in test, and guiding remediation (consolidation, cleanup, full sync). The engineer used Microsoft Graph queries to enumerate members of both the cloud-native and synchronized On‑Prem groups to compare differences in member lists. These investigative actions are documented in the entity and relationship records. [Data: Entities (261,271,269); Relationships (430,431)] The Entra ID portal was used to observe account "source" attributes (e.g., "source = Windows Server AD" vs "source = Azure AD"), providing direct evidence to distinguish which accounts were cloud-native guests and which were synchronized On‑Prem accounts. This combination of Graph API and portal inspection provided the necessary diagnostics for root-cause confirmation. [Data: Entities (270,267); Relationships (445,431)] ## Service dependency: SharePoint permission construct (SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN) referenced the COMPLIANCE_TEAM group, creating an access dependency SharePoint's permission construct SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN was found to contain or reference the COMPLIANCE_TEAM group; investigators examined membership mappings between these constructs during the analysis. Because SharePoint evaluated the group object that Azure AD Connect synchronized, the SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN -> COMPLIANCE_TEAM linkage is the downstream dependency that surfaced the access failure for external guests. [Data: Entities (264,265,266); Relationships (435)] This highlights that access control in SharePoint depended on the directory object identity (ObjectID) rather than purely on display name. Any change in which directory object is presented to SharePoint (for example, due to synchronization overwrite) will directly affect access evaluation for resources protected by SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN. [Data: Relationships (435,446)] ## Identity object collision risk: same display name, different ObjectIDs created a deterministic failure mode There were two distinct COMPLIANCE_TEAM objects: a cloud-native Azure AD group and an On‑Prem AD group synchronized by Azure AD Connect. Both used the same display name but had different ObjectIDs; when the synchronized On‑Prem object replaced or was used by downstream services, cloud-only members were excluded. This naming/collision pattern created a reproducible failure mode tied directly to synchronization behavior. [Data: Entities (265,266); Relationships (444,437,440)] Because Azure AD Connect will surface synchronized objects into Entra ID and object identity is the authoritative binding for resource access, organizations must treat display-name collisions between cloud-native and On‑Prem groups as high risk; this incident demonstrates how that risk materializes as access loss for cloud-only accounts. [Data: Relationships (446,437)] ## Remediation performed: consolidation, cleanup, and a full sync guided by the system engineer restored intended mappings The system engineer guided remediation actions including consolidation and cleanup of the conflicting group objects and performed a full sync to restore the correct group membership and access behavior. The ticket notes that a full synchronization was performed as part of remediation and that the engineer reproduced the issue in test to validate the fix. These documented steps indicate the operational path taken to remediate the outage. [Data: Entities (261,269); Relationships (430)] While the immediate remediation restored access, the records do not include a formal postmortem or a permanent governance control to prevent recurrence; the cleanup and full sync were corrective but the underlying naming/ownership risk remains unless addressed by policy. The available data supports the remediation actions but does not yet show a persistent mitigation mechanism. [Data: Entities (269,261); Relationships (430,437)] ## Governance and monitoring gaps: lack of explicit evidence of group ownership, naming policies, or sync exclusion rules The corpus documents the synchronization collision, diagnostic steps, and corrective actions, but it contains no explicit records describing group ownership assignments, naming conventions to prevent display-name collisions, or Azure AD Connect exclusion/filtering rules applied to groups to prevent cloud-native objects from being overwritten. The absence of these governance artifacts in the provided records indicates potential control gaps that allowed the condition to occur. [Data: Entities (265,266,269); Relationships (437,446,435)] Because SharePoint permission constructs referenced the group object identity and Azure AD Connect can alter which object is authoritative in the cloud, formal controls (group ownership, naming conventions, synchronization scoping/exclusions, and monitoring/alerting for object conflicts) are required to reduce recurrence risk. The immediate technical issues included misconfigured ADFS client registration parameters (EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) and delayed device state synchronization for hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices, which caused Conditional Access (Require compliant device for Finance Resources) to evaluate as not satisfied and block access for some users. Employee 21 authored consolidated ADFS configuration documentation, triggered manual device synchronization during remediation, and adjusted a Conditional Access rule (excluding a SharePoint project folder) to restore access; the same engineer also conducted WAF analysis that identified TLS inspection causing 502 errors and helped apply configuration adjustments that restored external access. The primary entities and relationships are: Employee 21 as the operational owner and author of the ADFS documentation, the ADFS CONFIGURATION DOCUMENTATION itself, the Finance Conditional Access policy, Hybrid Azure AD joined devices and the DEVICE SYNCHRONIZATION process, DEVICE COMPLIANCE STATUS results in Entra ID, and PRIVATE DEVICES which produced additional variability in access outcomes. [Data: Entities (232, 244, 680, 683, 684, 688, 685), Relationships (364, 1189, 1199, 1201, 1206, 1208, 365)] ## Single operational lead (Employee 21) conducted analysis, remediation, and documentation. Employee 21 served as the assigned ticket owner and lead analyst across multiple related incidents, performed remote troubleshooting, coordinated remediation with the Network Team, adjusted Conditional Access, triggered device synchronization, and authored the ADFS configuration notes and root‑cause analysis that identified misconfigured ADFS client registration parameters (EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) [Data: Entities (232), Relationships (1189, 365, 364)]. Having a single visible owner accelerated the investigation and produced formal documentation (ADFS CONFIGURATION DOCUMENTATION) and tactical remediations; this centralization is beneficial for rapid response but creates a single person dependency for institutional knowledge unless the documentation and handoff processes are formalized. The existence of authored documentation suggests an opportunity to convert incident knowledge into maintained runbooks and to ensure alternate owners are trained and assigned. ## Root cause for token/MFA failures: misconfigured ADFS client registration parameters. The documented root cause for the JWT/MFA login failures was ADFS client registration parameters (EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) being misconfigured; Employee 21 authored notes describing the corrected settings and consolidated those into a formal ADFS configuration document intended to align production to TEST‑environment values [Data: Entities (232, 244), Relationships (364)]. This indicates the authentication failure was caused by identity provider configuration drift rather than client or network connectivity alone. The formalization into ADFS CONFIGURATION DOCUMENTATION provides a basis for preventing recurrence if it is enforced via configuration management, automated checks, or periodic reviews against the documented expected values. ## Conditional Access policy interaction with device compliance blocked access to finance resources. The tenant Conditional Access policy titled 'Require compliant device for Finance Resources' evaluated device compliance as a condition; where device compliance status was 'unknown' or stale the policy evaluated as 'not satisfied', preventing access until compliance updated. The policy was adjusted (a SharePoint project folder excluded) as part of remediation to restore access for impacted users [Data: Entities (680, 232), Relationships (1201, 1199)]. This demonstrates a multi‑layered failure mode: identity provider configuration problems coincident with device compliance evaluation delays led to user impact for business‑critical (Finance) resources. The temporary exclusion of a resource from the CA policy is an appropriate tactical mitigation but should be followed by root cause fixes (device sync reliability and ADFS configuration) and a formal risk assessment to determine whether broader policy tuning or exceptions are warranted. ## Hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices experienced delayed/faulty synchronization, causing 'unknown' compliance and access failures. Hybrid Azure AD joined devices experienced faulty or delayed synchronization to Entra ID (6–8 hour lag), leaving device compliance status reported as 'unknown' and delaying Conditional Access evaluation and access restoration for some users. Employee 21 triggered manual device synchronization as part of remediation to update device compliance state faster [Data: Entities (683, 684, 688), Relationships (1206, 1189)]. Delayed device synchronization is a systemic reliability issue for hybrid device management that can repeatedly cause Conditional Access denials for otherwise compliant endpoints. Manual syncs are effective as an incident workaround but indicate the need for monitoring, alerting, and automation around device sync health, plus investigation into root causes (connectivity, AD‑connector health, sync throttling, or service limits) and capacity for remediation. ## Private/personal devices contributed to variability in access outcomes due to non‑managed compliance states. Some users accessed resources from private devices which are more likely to report non‑compliant or 'unknown' device status in Entra ID; this was observed in the incident and contributed to Conditional Access evaluation failures [Data: Entities (685, 688), Relationships (1208)]. Private devices introduce expected variability when CA policies require device compliance. The organization should treat private devices as higher‑risk by design (e.g., restrict finance resource access, require stronger controls, or use just‑in‑time access workflows) and ensure user education and policy clarity to reduce helpdesk load from predictable denial scenarios. ## WAF/TLS inspection caused 502 errors for external access; configuration adjustments restored service. During later work Employee 21 performed WAF analysis and identified TLS inspection as the cause of 502 errors when enabled; they compared the WAF configuration to the internal blueprint and helped apply configuration adjustments that restored external access [Data: Entities (232)]. This is an example of infrastructure security tooling (TLS inspection) interacting poorly with application traffic, producing availability issues. The finding underscores the need to validate WAF/inspection rules against blueprints and application behavior, include regression tests for TLS inspection changes, and maintain a rollback plan for inspection rules that disrupt production traffic. ## Documentation exists but should be operationalized into governance, monitoring, and runbooks. A consolidated ADFS CONFIGURATION DOCUMENTATION was recorded to capture the corrected ADFS client registration and investigation details; Employee 21 confirmed the configuration and notes have been documented to avoid future problems [Data: Entities (244), Relationships (364)]. Documentation is a necessary first step; to reduce operational risk this content should be converted into enforceable controls: baseline configuration templates, configuration drift detection (CI/CD or configuration management), automated compliance checks, runbooks for manual and automated remediation (including device sync triggers), and clearly mapped escalation/owner lists so incidents do not rely on a single individual's presence.

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 64: # Azure AD Connect vs Cloud COMPLIANCE_TEAM — External Guest Access Loss (SharePoint) This community centers on a cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM group and an On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM group that share a display name but have distinct ObjectIDs. Azure AD Connect synchronized the On‑Prem group into Entra ID/Azure AD, which progressively replaced or caused SharePoint to evaluate the synchronized On‑Prem object instead of the cloud-native group. Because external guest accounts lived only in the cloud-native group (they were not present On‑Prem), those external guests lost SharePoint access while internal users (present in the On‑Prem group) retained access. A system engineer reproduced the issue, used Microsoft Graph and the Entra ID portal to compare group membership and source attributes, and guided remediation steps including consolidation/cleanup and a full sync to restore intended access mappings. [Data: Entities (265,266,267,268,269,270); Relationships (437,440,441,445,446,435,430)] ## Root cause: Azure AD Connect synchronization caused the cloud-native group to be overwritten/evaluated as the On‑Prem synchronized object, removing external guests from effective membership Investigators observed that Azure AD Connect gradually overwrote or deactivated the cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM group with the synchronized On‑Prem group (same display name, different Object IDs), which changed the effective membership evaluated by SharePoint and resulted in external guests losing access. This behavior is documented in the service description for Azure AD Connect and the relationship between the cloud and On‑Prem group objects. [Data: Entities (269,265,266); Relationships (437,440,446)] The critical aspect is the display-name collision combined with synchronization semantics: SharePoint was referencing the synchronized On‑Prem object (the object with the On‑Prem ObjectID), not the separate cloud-native group that contained guest members, so membership evaluation excluded cloud-only guest accounts. This mismatch between object identity and display name is the immediate mechanism of access loss. [Data: Entities (265,266,267); Relationships (437,444,435)] ## Scope and impact: External guests lost access while internal users remained unaffected External project members provisioned as Guest accounts in Entra ID/Azure AD lost SharePoint access because they were members of the cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM, which was distinct from the synchronized On‑Prem group used by SharePoint. Internal users were members of the On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM and therefore continued to have access. This differential outcome is captured by the entity relationships showing guest membership in the cloud group and internal membership in the On‑Prem group. [Data: Entities (267,268,265,266); Relationships (437,441)] Investigators also noted that affected accounts had differing "source" attributes visible in the Entra ID portal; examining the source attribute helped correlate which accounts originated On‑Prem versus in the cloud, which was instrumental in identifying why some users were impacted and others were not. This observation ties the access symptoms back to identity source metadata in Entra ID. [Data: Entities (270,267); Relationships (445)] ## Investigation and diagnostics used: System Engineer 14, Microsoft Graph, and Entra ID inspection A system engineer conducted in-depth analysis: reviewing Azure AD Connect behavior, inspecting group membership via Microsoft Graph, reproducing the issue in test, and guiding remediation (consolidation, cleanup, full sync). The engineer used Microsoft Graph queries to enumerate members of both the cloud-native and synchronized On‑Prem groups to compare differences in member lists. These investigative actions are documented in the entity and relationship records. [Data: Entities (261,271,269); Relationships (430,431)] The Entra ID portal was used to observe account "source" attributes (e.g., "source = Windows Server AD" vs "source = Azure AD"), providing direct evidence to distinguish which accounts were cloud-native guests and which were synchronized On‑Prem accounts. This combination of Graph API and portal inspection provided the necessary diagnostics for root-cause confirmation. [Data: Entities (270,267); Relationships (445,431)] ## Service dependency: SharePoint permission construct (SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN) referenced the COMPLIANCE_TEAM group, creating an access dependency SharePoint's permission construct SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN was found to contain or reference the COMPLIANCE_TEAM group; investigators examined membership mappings between these constructs during the analysis. Because SharePoint evaluated the group object that Azure AD Connect synchronized, the SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN -> COMPLIANCE_TEAM linkage is the downstream dependency that surfaced the access failure for external guests. [Data: Entities (264,265,266); Relationships (435)] This highlights that access control in SharePoint depended on the directory object identity (ObjectID) rather than purely on display name. Any change in which directory object is presented to SharePoint (for example, due to synchronization overwrite) will directly affect access evaluation for resources protected by SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN. [Data: Relationships (435,446)] ## Identity object collision risk: same display name, different ObjectIDs created a deterministic failure mode There were two distinct COMPLIANCE_TEAM objects: a cloud-native Azure AD group and an On‑Prem AD group synchronized by Azure AD Connect. Both used the same display name but had different ObjectIDs; when the synchronized On‑Prem object replaced or was used by downstream services, cloud-only members were excluded. This naming/collision pattern created a reproducible failure mode tied directly to synchronization behavior. [Data: Entities (265,266); Relationships (444,437,440)] Because Azure AD Connect will surface synchronized objects into Entra ID and object identity is the authoritative binding for resource access, organizations must treat display-name collisions between cloud-native and On‑Prem groups as high risk; this incident demonstrates how that risk materializes as access loss for cloud-only accounts. [Data: Relationships (446,437)] ## Remediation performed: consolidation, cleanup, and a full sync guided by the system engineer restored intended mappings The system engineer guided remediation actions including consolidation and cleanup of the conflicting group objects and performed a full sync to restore the correct group membership and access behavior. The ticket notes that a full synchronization was performed as part of remediation and that the engineer reproduced the issue in test to validate the fix. These documented steps indicate the operational path taken to remediate the outage. [Data: Entities (261,269); Relationships (430)] While the immediate remediation restored access, the records do not include a formal postmortem or a permanent governance control to prevent recurrence; the cleanup and full sync were corrective but the underlying naming/ownership risk remains unless addressed by policy. The available data supports the remediation actions but does not yet show a persistent mitigation mechanism. [Data: Entities (269,261); Relationships (430,437)] ## Governance and monitoring gaps: lack of explicit evidence of group ownership, naming policies, or sync exclusion rules The corpus documents the synchronization collision, diagnostic steps, and corrective actions, but it contains no explicit records describing group ownership assignments, naming conventions to prevent display-name collisions, or Azure AD Connect exclusion/filtering rules applied to groups to prevent cloud-native objects from being overwritten. The absence of these governance artifacts in the provided records indicates potential control gaps that allowed the condition to occur. [Data: Entities (265,266,269); Relationships (437,446,435)] Because SharePoint permission constructs referenced the group object identity and Azure AD Connect can alter which object is authoritative in the cloud, formal controls (group ownership, naming conventions, synchronization scoping/exclusions, and monitoring/alerting for object conflicts) are required to reduce recurrence risk. The evidence of an operational incident plus lack of documented governance in the dataset supports elevating this as a priority for follow-up. [Data: Relationships (435,437,446)]

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 8: # SharePoint folder access — Sensitivity Label vs ACL (Customer Company 25 AG / Compliance_Review_2025) This community centers on a recurring SharePoint access problem in Customer Company 25 AG where a folder (/Projects/Compliance_Review_2025) was granted unique SharePoint ACLs, then later had a Microsoft Information Protection sensitivity label applied. The label enforced encryption tied to a different Azure AD group (Compliance_Core) while the SharePoint ACL referenced SP_Compliance_Main (which in turn referenced a cloud-created COMPLIANCE_TEAM). Azure AD Connect synchronization of an On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM object with the same display name (different ObjectID) replaced or changed the cloud object evaluation, excluding cloud-only external guests and producing access loss. Token caching and token-claim timing caused the symptom to appear intermittent (users could temporarily access with stale tokens, then lose access when tokens refreshed). The issue was reproduced in a TEST TENANT, investigated using Azure AD sign-in logs and Microsoft Purview, escalated through HELPDESK → BU Infrastructure (M365/Identity) → System Engineers, remediated by group consolidation / full sync / reapply label / invalidate tokens, and validated by the customer's Audit Team and Consulting Sample Company. ## Primary technical root cause: label encryption permissions diverged from SharePoint ACLs because the label was applied after unique permissions and 'Preserve custom permissions' was not enabled The folder /Projects/Compliance_Review_2025 had Unique Permissions (a SharePoint ACL) applied, and the Sensitivity Label ("Confidential – Internal") was applied later; because the label was applied without enabling the "Preserve custom permissions" option, the label's encryption referenced a different Azure AD group (Compliance_Core) than the ACL did, producing a permissions divergence that prevented decryption for some users. This sequence (ACL → label) and the missing preserve option are explicitly documented in the incident analysis and relationships examined during the investigation. This misalignment meant that SharePoint could show an ACL granting access (SP_Compliance_Main) while the label-based encryption check denied decryption because the token claims did not show membership in Compliance_Core. The label-enforced encryption thus became the effective gatekeeper for content access in practice, overriding the expectation set by the ACL alone. The incident record links the folder, the label configuration and the preserve-permissions option directly to the observed inconsistent access behavior. [Data: Entities (550, 571, 575, 556); Relationships (981, 947, 980)] ## Azure AD Connect group/object collision excluded external guests when the synchronized On‑Prem group object took precedence Investigators found a cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM group (containing external guest accounts) and a separate On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM group (with the same display name but a different Object ID). Azure AD Connect synchronization behavior caused the synchronized On‑Prem group object to overwrite or replace the cloud object reference used by SharePoint, which excluded cloud-only guest members and caused external guests to lose access when SharePoint evaluated the synchronized group object. System Engineer 14 and the identity team reproduced and explained this behaviour using Microsoft Graph queries and by reviewing Azure AD Connect behavior. The remediation included consolidation and a full sync to restore the intended cloud group membership. This synchronization/ID collision is the proximate cause that converted the ACL/label divergence into an actual user outage for external guests. [Data: Entities (269, 265, 266, 267); Relationships (446, 437, 430)] ## Token caching and claim timing produced intermittent/partial access symptoms Multiple records indicate that cached or previously-issued authentication tokens contained older group claims (e.g., COMPLIANCE_TEAM) that temporarily allowed access until tokens were refreshed or re-evaluated — at which point the absence of the Compliance_Core claim caused decryption to fail. Browser session state (including Incognito vs normal sessions) and Office client sessions retained tokens that influenced who saw the folder and when. Azure AD sign-in logs and token/claim records showed affected tokens contained COMPLIANCE_TEAM claims but lacked Compliance_Core; sign-in log timing and Conditional Access evaluation events correlated with observed intermittent behavior. Engineers therefore combined group/object fixes with token invalidation guidance (full sign‑out from Office + browser) to force reissuance of tokens with current claims. The cached-token behavior explains the seemingly sporadic nature of the access failures. [Data: Entities (681, 570, 558, 574); Relationships (991, 962, 1204)] ## Issue was reproducible in an isolated TEST TENANT and client-session tests showed cache-related differences Engineers reproduced the same ACL→label sequence in a TEST TENANT by creating a folder with unique permissions and applying a Sensitivity Label after the ACL was set; the test confirmed that token renewal and timing produced the same temporary-allowed/then-denied behavior. Browser Incognito testing produced different/partial access compared to normal sessions, further implicating cached session tokens and client caching as modifiers of symptom presentation. The use of a non-production test tenant allowed safe reproduction of the sequence and validation of remediation steps before applying them to production, and test findings (including incognito behavior) were used to justify the combined remediation approach (group consolidation + full sync + token invalidation + label reapplication). [Data: Entities (503, 567, 262); Relationships (993, 955, 996)] ## This is a recurrent, multi-ticket operational problem with demonstrated business impact Multiple support incidents across March–April 2025 reference similar SharePoint access issues for Customer Company 25 AG (tickets TI-1571046, TI-1578891, TI-1583327) and a related Teams voice ticket for a different customer (TI-1609823). The Customer Company 25 AG incidents specifically documented external project members losing access to project folders and required escalation to BU Infrastructure – M365/Identity and system engineers for in‑depth remediation. The recurrence and the number of escalations indicate a systemic interaction between label application processes, group/object management and synchronization rules that goes beyond a single, transient misconfiguration — and therefore warrants process and architecture changes to prevent repeat outages. [Data: Entities (546, 256, 676, 189); Relationships (419, 934, 1180, 288)] ## Stakeholder and escalation pathways were established and executed, but clarified ownership of label application and group governance is needed The support workflow shows HELPDESK as the frontline intake and triage point, escalating to BU Infrastructure – M365/Identity and then to System Engineers 11 and 14 for deeper analysis; Consulting Sample Company performed the investigation and remediation and the customer's Audit Team validated the final state. Communication paths (helpdesk ↔ contact persons, identity BU ↔ system engineers, consulting vendor ↔ customer) were used effectively to resolve the incidents. However, the root cause — interplay between SharePoint ACLs, sensitivity label configuration, and Azure AD group/object management — crosses service, identity and compliance boundaries. This cross-domain ownership means that explicit, codified governance is required for label application (who may apply labels and how), group naming/object‑ID collision handling, and Azure AD Connect change control in order to avoid reintroducing the same fault through routine administrative changes. [Data: Entities (85, 549, 261, 548, 568, 572); Relationships (297, 944, 967, 971)] ## Security/compliance configuration nuance: labels enforced encryption but ACL/label misalignment can deny intended principals Sensitivity Labels managed through Microsoft Purview/AIP enforce encryption that is evaluated against Azure AD group membership (e.g., requiring membership in Compliance_Core). The label mechanism is therefore capable of blocking decryption even when SharePoint ACLs indicate permission. In this incident, applying the label without preserving custom ACL mapping effectively changed the authority model for access, producing potential availability impacts for legitimate users and creating a risk that compliance-related labels could be misapplied in ways that disrupt business processes. The investigation used Purview tooling to review label configuration and concluded that label application options (such as "Preserve custom permissions") materially affect access outcomes; thus label application must be treated as both a security and availability control, and require pre‑application checks and a rollback/test path. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 8: # SharePoint folder access — Sensitivity Label vs ACL (Customer Company 25 AG / Compliance_Review_2025) This community centers on a recurring SharePoint access problem in Customer Company 25 AG where a folder (/Projects/Compliance_Review_2025) was granted unique SharePoint ACLs, then later had a Microsoft Information Protection sensitivity label applied. The label enforced encryption tied to a different Azure AD group (Compliance_Core) while the SharePoint ACL referenced SP_Compliance_Main (which in turn referenced a cloud-created COMPLIANCE_TEAM). Azure AD Connect synchronization of an On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM object with the same display name (different ObjectID) replaced or changed the cloud object evaluation, excluding cloud-only external guests and producing access loss. Token caching and token-claim timing caused the symptom to appear intermittent (users could temporarily access with stale tokens, then lose access when tokens refreshed). The issue was reproduced in a TEST TENANT, investigated using Azure AD sign-in logs and Microsoft Purview, escalated through HELPDESK → BU Infrastructure (M365/Identity) → System Engineers, remediated by group consolidation / full sync / reapply label / invalidate tokens, and validated by the customer's Audit Team and Consulting Sample Company. ## Primary technical root cause: label encryption permissions diverged from SharePoint ACLs because the label was applied after unique permissions and 'Preserve custom permissions' was not enabled The folder /Projects/Compliance_Review_2025 had Unique Permissions (a SharePoint ACL) applied, and the Sensitivity Label ("Confidential – Internal") was applied later; because the label was applied without enabling the "Preserve custom permissions" option, the label's encryption referenced a different Azure AD group (Compliance_Core) than the ACL did, producing a permissions divergence that prevented decryption for some users. This sequence (ACL → label) and the missing preserve option are explicitly documented in the incident analysis and relationships examined during the investigation. This misalignment meant that SharePoint could show an ACL granting access (SP_Compliance_Main) while the label-based encryption check denied decryption because the token claims did not show membership in Compliance_Core. The label-enforced encryption thus became the effective gatekeeper for content access in practice, overriding the expectation set by the ACL alone. The incident record links the folder, the label configuration and the preserve-permissions option directly to the observed inconsistent access behavior. [Data: Entities (550, 571, 575, 556); Relationships (981, 947, 980)] ## Azure AD Connect group/object collision excluded external guests when the synchronized On‑Prem group object took precedence Investigators found a cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM group (containing external guest accounts) and a separate On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM group (with the same display name but a different Object ID). Azure AD Connect synchronization behavior caused the synchronized On‑Prem group object to overwrite or replace the cloud object reference used by SharePoint, which excluded cloud-only guest members and caused external guests to lose access when SharePoint evaluated the synchronized group object. System Engineer 14 and the identity team reproduced and explained this behaviour using Microsoft Graph queries and by reviewing Azure AD Connect behavior. The remediation included consolidation and a full sync to restore the intended cloud group membership. This synchronization/ID collision is the proximate cause that converted the ACL/label divergence into an actual user outage for external guests. [Data: Entities (269, 265, 266, 267); Relationships (446, 437, 430)] ## Token caching and claim timing produced intermittent/partial access symptoms Multiple records indicate that cached or previously-issued authentication tokens contained older group claims (e.g., COMPLIANCE_TEAM) that temporarily allowed access until tokens were refreshed or re-evaluated — at which point the absence of the Compliance_Core claim caused decryption to fail. Browser session state (including Incognito vs normal sessions) and Office client sessions retained tokens that influenced who saw the folder and when. Azure AD sign-in logs and token/claim records showed affected tokens contained COMPLIANCE_TEAM claims but lacked Compliance_Core; sign-in log timing and Conditional Access evaluation events correlated with observed intermittent behavior. Engineers therefore combined group/object fixes with token invalidation guidance (full sign‑out from Office + browser) to force reissuance of tokens with current claims. The cached-token behavior explains the seemingly sporadic nature of the access failures. [Data: Entities (681, 570, 558, 574); Relationships (991, 962, 1204)] ## Issue was reproducible in an isolated TEST TENANT and client-session tests showed cache-related differences Engineers reproduced the same ACL→label sequence in a TEST TENANT by creating a folder with unique permissions and applying a Sensitivity Label after the ACL was set; the test confirmed that token renewal and timing produced the same temporary-allowed/then-denied behavior. Browser Incognito testing produced different/partial access compared to normal sessions, further implicating cached session tokens and client caching as modifiers of symptom presentation. The use of a non-production test tenant allowed safe reproduction of the sequence and validation of remediation steps before applying them to production, and test findings (including incognito behavior) were used to justify the combined remediation approach (group consolidation + full sync + token invalidation + label reapplication). [Data: Entities (503, 567, 262); Relationships (993, 955, 996)] ## This is a recurrent, multi-ticket operational problem with demonstrated business impact Multiple support incidents across March–April 2025 reference similar SharePoint access issues for Customer Company 25 AG (tickets TI-1571046, TI-1578891, TI-1583327) and a related Teams voice ticket for a different customer (TI-1609823). The Customer Company 25 AG incidents specifically documented external project members losing access to project folders and required escalation to BU Infrastructure – M365/Identity and system engineers for in‑depth remediation. The recurrence and the number of escalations indicate a systemic interaction between label application processes, group/object management and synchronization rules that goes beyond a single, transient misconfiguration — and therefore warrants process and architecture changes to prevent repeat outages. [Data: Entities (546, 256, 676, 189); Relationships (419, 934, 1180, 288)] ## Stakeholder and escalation pathways were established and executed, but clarified ownership of label application and group governance is needed The support workflow shows HELPDESK as the frontline intake and triage point, escalating to BU Infrastructure – M365/Identity and then to System Engineers 11 and 14 for deeper analysis; Consulting Sample Company performed the investigation and remediation and the customer's Audit Team validated the final state. Communication paths (helpdesk ↔ contact persons, identity BU ↔ system engineers, consulting vendor ↔ customer) were used effectively to resolve the incidents. However, the root cause — interplay between SharePoint ACLs, sensitivity label configuration, and Azure AD group/object management — crosses service, identity and compliance boundaries. This cross-domain ownership means that explicit, codified governance is required for label application (who may apply labels and how), group naming/object‑ID collision handling, and Azure AD Connect change control in order to avoid reintroducing the same fault through routine administrative changes. [Data: Entities (85, 549, 261, 548, 568, 572); Relationships (297, 944, 967, 971)] ## Security/compliance configuration nuance: labels enforced encryption but ACL/label misalignment can deny intended principals Sensitivity Labels managed through Microsoft Purview/AIP enforce encryption that is evaluated against Azure AD group membership (e.g., requiring membership in Compliance_Core). The label mechanism is therefore capable of blocking decryption even when SharePoint ACLs indicate permission. In this incident, applying the label without preserving custom ACL mapping effectively changed the authority model for access, producing potential availability impacts for legitimate users and creating a risk that compliance-related labels could be misapplied in ways that disrupt business processes. The investigation used Purview tooling to review label configuration and concluded that label application options (such as "Preserve custom permissions") materially affect access outcomes; thus label application must be treated as both a security and availability control, and require pre‑application checks and a rollback/test path. [Data: Entities (557, 559, 556); Relationships (977, 959, 981)] ## Prioritized, actionable recommendations to prevent recurrence Based on the documented causes and remediation steps, the following prioritized actions are indicated: (1) Treat label application as a controlled change — enable "Preserve custom permissions" or explicitly map ACL → label group mappings and test in a TEST TENANT prior to production; (2) Implement Azure AD Connect governance: detect display-name collisions, require ObjectID reconciliation/explicit mapping for groups with identical display names, and use pre-sync validation to prevent cloud group overwrite; (3) Add a runbook to force token revocation and clear Office/browser sessions after any group or label change (sign‑out guidance, token revocation, and customer communication); (4) Maintain a pre-label checklist (verify group referenced by label exists and contains intended principals including guests) and require Purview review/approval for applied encryption labels; (5) Add monitoring/alerts for rapid detection: track label application events, group-object replacements, and anomalous sign-in errors referencing encryption failures. Each recommended action maps directly to evidence and remediation steps recorded in the incident (preserve permissions option, Azure AD Connect behavior, test-tenant verification, token invalidation guidance, and Purview analysis) and should be implemented in the order of operational impact and ease of deployment (preserve-permissions / pre-application tests first, then AAD Connect governance and automation). Observation: Group “COMPLIANCE_TEAM” exists both On-Prem and in Cloud. On-Prem version is synchronized. Cloud version was originally created manually. Result: • External guests exist only in Cloud group • SharePoint references the synchronized On-Prem group Same name, but different Object IDs. 10.04.25 11:25 Review of group members via Graph: Cloud group: → Contains external guests Synchronized On-Prem group: → Contains only internal users Since SharePoint evaluates by Object ID, not by name, external users lose access once synchronization takes effect. 11.04.25 08:30 Reproduction in test: • On-Prem group re-synchronized • Cloud group overwritten • External users reproducibly lose access Root Cause Two groups existed with identical name: COMPLIANCE_TEAM 1. Cloud-native group 2. Synchronized On-Prem group Through Azure AD Connect, the Cloud group was gradually overwritten or deactivated. SharePoint referenced the synchronized version. External guests were never managed On-Prem and therefore were not included in the synchronized group. Actions 11.04.25 – 14.04.25 • Consolidation of group structure • Old Cloud group removed • On-Prem group properly cleaned • External guests managed as Cloud-only members • Azure AD Connect full synchronization performed 15.04.25 – Monitoring Access remains stable No further inconsistencies 17.04.25 15:18 – Ticket closure Dear Contact Person 07 The root cause was the parallel existence of two groups with identical names but different origins (Cloud vs. On-Prem synchronized). Due to synchronization, effective group membership for external users was not correctly applied. The group structure was consolidated and fully synchronized. Access is now stable. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company

Assigned Group changed to: BU Infrastructure – M365 / Identity 22.03.25 09:12 Ticket taken over by: System Engineer 11 Status: In Progress 22.03.25 10:25 Analysis by System Engineer 11 Step 1 – Sensitivity Label review Label “Confidential – Internal” checked: • Encryption active • Access only for members of Azure AD group “Compliance_Core” • Automatic inheritance for folders enabled Notable: Folder “Compliance_Review_2025” has Unique Permissions. Label was applied AFTER renaming. Hypothesis: Conflict between SharePoint permissions and AIP label encryption. 22.03.25 11:40 Test: • User added directly to “Compliance_Core” → Access immediately possible After removal from “Compliance_Core” → Access denied again However: Other users WITHOUT “Compliance_Core” membership have access. Hypothesis rejected – not purely group-based. 24.03.25 08:55 Analysis Azure AD Sign-In Logs For affected users: • Token contains claim for “COMPLIANCE_TEAM” • No claim for “Compliance_Core” • Conditional Access evaluation successful No blocking visible. 24.03.25 10:10 In-depth review via Microsoft Purview / Information Protection REPORT 8: # SharePoint folder access — Sensitivity Label vs ACL (Customer Company 25 AG / Compliance_Review_2025) This community centers on a recurring SharePoint access problem in Customer Company 25 AG where a folder (/Projects/Compliance_Review_2025) was granted unique SharePoint ACLs, then later had a Microsoft Information Protection sensitivity label applied. The label enforced encryption tied to a different Azure AD group (Compliance_Core) while the SharePoint ACL referenced SP_Compliance_Main (which in turn referenced a cloud-created COMPLIANCE_TEAM). Azure AD Connect synchronization of an On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM object with the same display name (different ObjectID) replaced or changed the cloud object evaluation, excluding cloud-only external guests and producing access loss. Token caching and token-claim timing caused the symptom to appear intermittent (users could temporarily access with stale tokens, then lose access when tokens refreshed). The issue was reproduced in a TEST TENANT, investigated using Azure AD sign-in logs and Microsoft Purview, escalated through HELPDESK → BU Infrastructure (M365/Identity) → System Engineers, remediated by group consolidation / full sync / reapply label / invalidate tokens, and validated by the customer's Audit Team and Consulting Sample Company. ## Primary technical root cause: label encryption permissions diverged from SharePoint ACLs because the label was applied after unique permissions and 'Preserve custom permissions' was not enabled The folder /Projects/Compliance_Review_2025 had Unique Permissions (a SharePoint ACL) applied, and the Sensitivity Label ("Confidential – Internal") was applied later; because the label was applied without enabling the "Preserve custom permissions" option, the label's encryption referenced a different Azure AD group (Compliance_Core) than the ACL did, producing a permissions divergence that prevented decryption for some users. This sequence (ACL → label) and the missing preserve option are explicitly documented in the incident analysis and relationships examined during the investigation. This misalignment meant that SharePoint could show an ACL granting access (SP_Compliance_Main) while the label-based encryption check denied decryption because the token claims did not show membership in Compliance_Core. The label-enforced encryption thus became the effective gatekeeper for content access in practice, overriding the expectation set by the ACL alone. The incident record links the folder, the label configuration and the preserve-permissions option directly to the observed inconsistent access behavior. [Data: Entities (550, 571, 575, 556); Relationships (981, 947, 980)] ## Azure AD Connect group/object collision excluded external guests when the synchronized On‑Prem group object took precedence Investigators found a cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM group (containing external guest accounts) and a separate On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM group (with the same display name but a different Object ID). Azure AD Connect synchronization behavior caused the synchronized On‑Prem group object to overwrite or replace the cloud object reference used by SharePoint, which excluded cloud-only guest members and caused external guests to lose access when SharePoint evaluated the synchronized group object. System Engineer 14 and the identity team reproduced and explained this behaviour using Microsoft Graph queries and by reviewing Azure AD Connect behavior. The remediation included consolidation and a full sync to restore the intended cloud group membership. This synchronization/ID collision is the proximate cause that converted the ACL/label divergence into an actual user outage for external guests. [Data: Entities (269, 265, 266, 267); Relationships (446, 437, 430)] ## Token caching and claim timing produced intermittent/partial access symptoms Multiple records indicate that cached or previously-issued authentication tokens contained older group claims (e.g., COMPLIANCE_TEAM) that temporarily allowed access until tokens were refreshed or re-evaluated — at which point the absence of the Compliance_Core claim caused decryption to fail. Browser session state (including Incognito vs normal sessions) and Office client sessions retained tokens that influenced who saw the folder and when. Azure AD sign-in logs and token/claim records showed affected tokens contained COMPLIANCE_TEAM claims but lacked Compliance_Core; sign-in log timing and Conditional Access evaluation events correlated with observed intermittent behavior. Engineers therefore combined group/object fixes with token invalidation guidance (full sign‑out from Office + browser) to force reissuance of tokens with current claims. The cached-token behavior explains the seemingly sporadic nature of the access failures. [Data: Entities (681, 570, 558, 574); Relationships (991, 962, 1204)] ## Issue was reproducible in an isolated TEST TENANT and client-session tests showed cache-related differences Engineers reproduced the same ACL→label sequence in a TEST TENANT by creating a folder with unique permissions and applying a Sensitivity Label after the ACL was set; the test confirmed that token renewal and timing produced the same temporary-allowed/then-denied behavior. Browser Incognito testing produced different/partial access compared to normal sessions, further implicating cached session tokens and client caching as modifiers of symptom presentation. The use of a non-production test tenant allowed safe reproduction of the sequence and validation of remediation steps before applying them to production, and test findings (including incognito behavior) were used to justify the combined remediation approach (group consolidation + full sync + token invalidation + label reapplication). [Data: Entities (503, 567, 262); Relationships (993, 955, 996)] ## This is a recurrent, multi-ticket operational problem with demonstrated business impact Multiple support incidents across March–April 2025 reference similar SharePoint access issues for Customer Company 25 AG (tickets TI-1571046, TI-1578891, TI-1583327) and a related Teams voice ticket for a different customer (TI-1609823). The Customer Company 25 AG incidents specifically documented external project members losing access to project folders and required escalation to BU Infrastructure – M365/Identity and system engineers for in‑depth remediation. The recurrence and the number of escalations indicate a systemic interaction between label application processes, group/object management and synchronization rules that goes beyond a single, transient misconfiguration — and therefore warrants process and architecture changes to prevent repeat outages. [Data: Entities (546, 256, 676, 189); Relationships (419, 934, 1180, 288)] ## Stakeholder and escalation pathways were established and executed, but clarified ownership of label application and group governance is needed The support workflow shows HELPDESK as the frontline intake and triage point, escalating to BU Infrastructure – M365/Identity and then to System Engineers 11 and 14 for deeper analysis; Consulting Sample Company performed the investigation and remediation and the customer's Audit Team validated the final state. Communication paths (helpdesk ↔ contact persons, identity BU ↔ system engineers, consulting vendor ↔ customer) were used effectively to resolve the incidents. However, the root cause — interplay between SharePoint ACLs, sensitivity label configuration, and Azure AD group/object management — crosses service, identity and compliance boundaries. This cross-domain ownership means that explicit, codified governance is required for label application (who may apply labels and how), group naming/object‑ID collision handling, and Azure AD Connect change control in order to avoid reintroducing the same fault through routine administrative changes. [Data: Entities (85, 549, 261, 548, 568, 572); Relationships (297, 944, 967, 971)] ## Security/compliance configuration nuance: labels enforced encryption but ACL/label misalignment can deny intended principals Sensitivity Labels managed through Microsoft Purview/AIP enforce encryption that is evaluated against Azure AD group membership (e.g., requiring membership in Compliance_Core). The label mechanism is therefore capable of blocking decryption even when SharePoint ACLs indicate permission. In this incident, applying the label without preserving custom ACL mapping effectively changed the authority model for access, producing potential availability impacts for legitimate users and creating a risk that compliance-related labels could be misapplied in ways that disrupt business processes. The investigation used Purview tooling to review label configuration and concluded that label application options (such as "Preserve custom permissions") materially affect access outcomes; thus label application must be treated as both a security and availability control, and require pre‑application checks and a rollback/test path. [Data: Entities (557, 559, 556); Relationships (977, 959, 981)] ## Prioritized, actionable recommendations to prevent recurrence Based on the documented causes and remediation steps, the following prioritized actions are indicated: (1) Treat label application as a controlled change — enable "Preserve custom permissions" or explicitly map ACL → label group mappings and test in a TEST TENANT prior to production; (2) Implement Azure AD Connect governance: detect display-name collisions, require ObjectID reconciliation/explicit mapping for groups with identical display names, and use pre-sync validation to prevent cloud group overwrite; (3) Add a runbook to force token revocation and clear Office/browser sessions after any group or label change (sign‑out guidance, token revocation, and customer communication); (4) Maintain a pre-label checklist (verify group referenced by label exists and contains intended principals including guests) and require Purview review/approval for applied encryption labels; (5) Add monitoring/alerts for rapid detection: track label application events, group-object replacements, and anomalous sign-in errors referencing encryption failures. Each recommended action maps directly to evidence and remediation steps recorded in the incident (preserve permissions option, Azure AD Connect behavior, test-tenant verification, token invalidation guidance, and Purview analysis) and should be implemented in the order of operational impact and ease of deployment (preserve-permissions / pre-application tests first, then AAD Connect governance and automation). [Data: Entities (575, 269, 503, 681, 559); Relationships (980, 446, 993, 1204, 940)] REPORT 13: # KF40 Portal Publication (portal.kf40.ch) — WAF/Reverse-Proxy and Firewall Operational Community This community centers on the perimeter publishing stack (WAF / Reverse Proxy) that publishes the Customer Company 40 portal (portal.kf40.ch) and the network/firewall estate that supports user connectivity (including Teams media). A high‑priority support ticket (TI-1594408) documented an external 502 Bad Gateway outage for portal.kf40.ch while internal access remained functional. Troubleshooting showed the backend application servers and load balancer were healthy, TLS certificates were validated and not the root cause, and the outage correlated to a weekend security patch/change that reloaded a Default-Strict inspection/profile and removed a custom header rewrite rule needed to present X-Forwarded-Proto: https to the backend. Restoring the Portal-App-Template / custom header rewrite configuration per the Secure Web Publishing blueprint resolved external access. Separately, a Zurich site firewall hardening change restricted outbound UDP to ports 3478–3481 only, blocking the dynamic RTP port range and causing one‑way/no audio in Teams; a temporary port opening restored media flows and confirmed the root cause. The incident and artifacts highlight technical, process, and governance gaps around post‑patch template/profile validation, change control for perimeter devices, and runbooks for header/HTTP profile preservation during TLS inspection/termination. ## Root cause of portal 502: WAF TLS inspection plus missing/custom header rewrite rule (X-Forwarded-Proto) prevented the backend from seeing HTTPS. External requests to portal.kf40.ch returned 502 Bad Gateway while internal access remained functional; investigation showed the reverse proxy/WAF performs TLS termination/inspection and is expected to forward X-Forwarded-Proto: https to the backend, but that header was not present when inspection was active, causing the backend to misinterpret the request and return errors. This causal chain is documented in the incident artifacts linking the REVERSE PROXY / WAF to the APPLICATION SERVER and noting the missing CUSTOM HEADER REWRITE RULE and absent X-FORWARDED-PROTO header as drivers for the 502s [Data: Entities (302,312,319); Relationships (492,499,496)]. Restoring the header rewrite behavior per the Portal-App-Template/blueprint returned external functionality, confirming the header rewrite absence (not the backend or certificate validity) was the functional trigger for failures [Data: Entities (311,300); Relationships (568,499)]. ## Security patch/template reload replaced Portal-App-Template with Default-Strict, causing template/profile drift and header removal. A weekend security patch caused the reverse proxy/WAF to reload a default inspection/profile (Default-Strict) which replaced the custom PORTAL-APP-TEMPLATE and its documented HTTP/profile/header‑rewrite rules; the activated Default-Strict profile altered header-handling and removed or failed to preserve X-Forwarded headers, reproducing the 502 when TLS inspection was enabled [Data: Entities (310,311); Relationships (497,568,548)]. This shows configuration/template drift introduced by patching—where a device reset to a default profile overwrote application‑specific templates—rather than an application or certificate error, creating a clear process risk for perimeter devices that host per-application templates [Data: Relationships (497,562)]. ## Backend and load‑balancing layer were healthy—issue isolated to publishing/inspection layer. Load balancer health checks and internal tests marked the backend application server as healthy and reachable, indicating the backend process was not the primary failure point; monitoring and health checks were used to validate stability after the fix before ticket closure [Data: Entities (305,318,308); Relationships (504,505,513)]. These diagnostics materially constrained the fault domain to the perimeter (WAF/reverse proxy) and saved time by directing remediation to template/profile/header handling rather than application restarts alone [Data: Relationships (492,563)]. ## TLS certificate change was investigated and ruled out as the root cause. The customer reported a recently installed TLS certificate and helpdesk validated the certificate CN, SAN and chain; while the certificate change was temporally proximate to the outage, diagnostics showed certificate validity/chain issues were not the cause of the 502s—the publishing layer header behavior was [Data: Entities (306); Relationships (486)]. The certificate service and blueprint decision tree were consulted as part of the normal checklist, which helped rule TLS problems out and focus on WAF behavior [Data: Relationships (552,508)]. ## Teams media outage at Zurich caused by outbound firewall restriction blocking dynamic RTP ports. A separate but contemporaneous operational change at the Zurich site implemented a security-hardening outbound rule that permitted only UDP 3478–3481, blocking the dynamic RTP port range required for Teams media when Media Bypass is used. This change produced no audio for external calls; temporarily opening the wider UDP/RTP range restored audio immediately, confirming the firewall rule as the root cause [Data: Entities (303,442,443); Relationships (739,741,742,733)]. Firewall logs and change records tied the timing of the outbound restriction to the onset of audio issues, showing this was a local change management failure with direct impact to voice/media availability [Data: Relationships (738,739)]. ## Blueprint and operational docs exist but were not enforced by the post‑patch validation process. An authoritative Secure Web Publishing blueprint and versioned guidance (BLUEPRINT: SECURE WEB PUBLISHING – KF40 – VERSION 3.2 / WEB PUBLISHING BLUEPRINT) defines required behaviors: TLS termination at the WAF, required Portal-App-Template, and documented header rewrite rules and validation after patches. The team retrieved the blueprint from DMS to align remediation, indicating awareness of the standard; however, the blueprint's requirement to validate WAF profiles and preserve custom rewrite rules was not enforced automatically during the patch that reset profiles, producing configuration drift [Data: Entities (307,339,356); Relationships (508,512,562)]. This gap between blueprint and operational enforcement—where a profile reload caused a prohibited Default-Strict to be applied despite the blueprint forbidding it—exposes the environment to repeat regressions unless validation and change controls are hardened [Data: Relationships (548,497)]. ## Operational ownership, escalation path, and remediation were used correctly but could be formalized into runbooks and automated checks. The high-priority ticket TI-1594408 was owned initially by Employee 29 (helpdesk) who performed initial checks and escalated to BU Network & Security; BU Network & Security then took ownership and applied WAF/reverse proxy configuration changes to restore service, with monitoring confirming 24 hours stability before closure—showing correct functional escalation and remediation occurred [Data: Entities (295,298,299); Relationships (479,487,476)]. The artifacts show manual steps and handoffs (tests, template restores, monitoring) that resolved the incident, but the manual nature of these actions recommends formalizing a post‑patch WAF validation runbook, automated configuration drift detection, and explicit pre/post patch checklists to reduce mean‑time‑to‑repair and recurrence risk [Data: Relationships (479,513)].
